lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Why should I play the Roman fool, and die

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: On mine own sword? whiles I see lives, the gashes

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Do better upon them.

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Turn, hell-hound, turn!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Of all men else I have avoided thee:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: But get thee back; my soul is too much charged

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: With blood of thine already.

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: I have no words:

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: My voice is in my sword: thou bloodier villain

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Than terms can give thee out! They fight.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thou losest labour:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: As easy mayst thou the intrenchant air

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: With thy keen sword impress as make me bleed:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Let fall thy blade on vulnerable crests;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I bear a charmed life, which must not yield

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To one of woman born.

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Despair thy charm;

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: And let the angel whom thou still hast served

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Tell thee, Macduff was from his mother’s womb

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Untimely ripp’d.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Accursed be that tongue that tells me so,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: For it hath cow’d my better part of man!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And be these juggling fiends no more believed,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That palter with us in a double sense;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That keep the word of promise to our ear,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And break it to our hope. I’ll not fight with thee.

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Then yield thee, coward,

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: And live to be the show and gaze o’ the time:

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: We’ll have thee, as our rarer monsters are,

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Painted on a pole, and underwrit,

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: “Here may you see the tyrant.”

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I will not yield,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To kiss the ground before young Malcolm’s feet,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And to be baited with the rabble’s curse.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Though Birnam wood be come to Dunsinane,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And thou opposed, being of no woman born,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Yet I will try the last. Before my body

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I throw my warlike shield. Lay on, Macduff,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And damn’d be him that first cries, “Hold, enough!”

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: I would the friends we miss were safe arrived.

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: Some must go off: and yet, by these I see,

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: So great a day as this is cheaply bought.

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Macduff is missing, and your noble son.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Your son, my lord, has paid a soldier’s debt:

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: He only lived but till he was a man;

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: The which no sooner had his prowess confirm’d

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: In the unshrinking station where he fought,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: But like a man he died.

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: Then he is dead?

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Ay, and brought off the field: your cause of sorrow

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Must not be measured by his worth, for then

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: It hath no end.

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: Had he his hurts before?

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Ay, on the front.

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: Why then, God’s soldier be he!

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: Had I as many sons as I have hairs,

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: I would not wish them to a fairer death:

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: And so, his knell is knoll’d.

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: He’s worth more sorrow,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: And that I’ll spend for him.

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: He’s worth no more:

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: They say he parted well, and paid his score:

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: And so, God be with him! Here comes newer comfort.

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Hail, king! for so thou art: behold, where stands

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: The usurper’s cursed head: the time is free:

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: I see thee compass’d with thy kingdom’s pearl,

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: That speak my salutation in their minds;

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Whose voices I desire aloud with mine:

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Hail, King of Scotland!

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Hail, King of Scotland!

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: We shall not spend a large expense of time

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Before we reckon with your several loves,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: And make us even with you. My thanes and kinsmen,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Henceforth be earls, the first that ever Scotland

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: In such an honour named. What’s more to do,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Which would be planted newly with the time,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: As calling home our exiled friends abroad

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: That fled the snares of watchful tyranny;

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Producing forth the cruel ministers

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Of this dead butcher and his fiend-like queen,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Who, as ’tis thought, by self and violent hands

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Took off her life; this, and what needful else

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: That calls upon us, by the grace of Grace,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: We will perform in measure, time and place:

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: So, thanks to all at once and to each one,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Whom we invite to see us crown’d at Scone.

stage_directions.text: Retreat. Flourish. Enter, with drum and colours, Malcolm, old Siward, Ross, the other Thanes, and Soldiers.

glosses.definition: a Stoic suicide in the classical manner — Macbeth rejects suicide
air that cannot be cut — Macbeth's bravado about his charmed life
heads that can be hurt (i.e., other men)
Macbeth's still-confident boast — about to be shattered
Macduff's revelation — give up believing in the charm

glosses.phrase: angel whom thou still hast served
glosses.definition: the dark angel (devil) Macbeth has served — called to give him the truth
cut out before birth (by Caesarean section)
the Witches — deceitful devils who play with words

glosses.phrase: palter with us in a double sense

glosses.definition: strike — Macbeth's final command to his killer
by the grace of God — the new reign will be godly
the coronation-site of Scottish kings — the new reign begins where 2.4 left off

annotations.title: The equivocation sprung
annotations.body:
Macbeth's confidence in the Second Apparition's prophecy breaks in a single exchange. 'I bear a charmed life, which must not yield / To one of woman born.' Macduff: 'Despair thy charm; / And let the angel whom thou still hast served / Tell thee, Macduff was from his mother's womb / Untimely ripp'd.' Caesarean birth — which early-modern medicine categorized as not, strictly, 'born' in the ordinary sense — has made Macduff the prophecy's loophole.

Macbeth's response — 'these juggling fiends no more believed, / That palter with us in a double sense' — is the play's closing indictment of equivocation. The Porter's equivocator from 2.3, the Witches' double meanings from 4.1, Lady Macbeth's 'serpent under the flower' from 1.5 — all have combined to produce this defeat. Macbeth dies knowing he has been played by his own technique.

His last line (Lay on, Macduff; / And damn'd be him that first cries, Hold, enough!) recovers something. He has lost everything, but he will not surrender. The soldier Macbeth of 1.2 returns in the last couplet. It is not redemption — but it is a kind of closing coherence.

annotations.title: Malcolm's coronation and the new earldoms
annotations.body:
Malcolm's coronation speech makes Scotland's Thanes into 'earls' — the first earls in Scottish history, per Holinshed. The play ends with an administrative innovation: Scotland will be reorganized on the English model. Macduff, not Malcolm, delivers the line naming Macbeth 'this dead butcher and his fiend-like queen,' setting the official verdict.

The play's final couplet belongs to the restored state: 'So thanks to all at once and to each one, / Whom we invite to see us crown'd at Scone.' Scone — the coronation site first mentioned in 2.4 as the site of Macbeth's crowning — now receives Malcolm. The play has taken the audience from Duncan's body (2.3) to Macbeth's crowning (2.4, offstage) to Malcolm's crowning (5.8, also offstage). Three kings, one throne, one site. The political order has been cycled through rupture and restoration.

The audience of 1606 would have read the conclusion as confirmation of the Stuart legitimacy theme: disorder is chaotic but temporary; rightful kingship returns. It was what James I wanted the play to mean. It is also what the play means. The two are not incompatible.

options: Macduff's revelation that he was 'from his mother's womb untimely ripp'd' — Caesarean birth is the prophecy's loophole

trials.prompt: Macbeth's last line ('Lay on, Macduff') recovers:
options: The soldier of 1.2 — not redemption, but closing coherence; he refuses to surrender

trials.prompt: Malcolm's creation of 'earls' at the end:
options: Is historical — per Holinshed, the first earls in Scottish history, reorganizing Scotland on the English model
